refs.note: "in the previous hearing, from the testimony of Heraclius the Syracusan" --- the actio prima.
"You could understand in the previous hearing" --- the testimony of Roman citizens from Sicily.
Dio's testimony at the actio prima, that one million sesterces was paid to Verres.
"Under the same praetor as Annia, daughter of senator P. Annius": back-reference to the inheritance edict episode of Book 1.
Reads testimonies of Lucullus, Chlorus, Dion delivered at the actio prima.

The Africanus anecdote ("Do not wonder if you do not obtain this from me") is a moralizing chreia type, possibly from a published source on Scipio (e.g. Polybius) but presented here as common knowledge.
Metellus's reversals reported at the actio prima.
Heraclius of Centuripa's testimony in the previous hearing.
Timarchides as already known from "very many witnesses" at the actio prima.
The figures Bulbus and Staienus link forward to the bribe-broker scandal at the heart of Pro Cluentio (66 BC).
Testimony of Sextus Pompeius Chlorus from the previous hearing.

The Cn. Dolabella prosecution (78/77 BC) is the precedent for rescinding in-absence condemnations: Philodamus of Opus's case was reopened then. Cicero treats it as a settled matter of judicial precedent.
The Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus / M. Silanus / Aegritomarus prosecution (c. 109 BC) is the precedent for crossing class to prosecute on a guest-friend's behalf.
Testimonies of envoys from Centuripa, Halaesa, Catina, Panhormus at the actio prima.
Timarchides's plundering testified to in the previous hearing by many.
Hortensius's quip in the previous hearing about Artemo of Centuripa being "a prosecutor, not a witness."
Carpinatius and the scriptura partnership become a major thread in Book 2-3 (the tithe-and-grain charges).
